  • Hide All Code

Department of Public Works and Highways

Long-run appropriations and the geography of realignments, FY 1988–2026

June 2026

← All agencies

Overview

This document profiles the budget of the Department of Public Works and Highways (DPWH) — the government’s largest infrastructure agency — from two angles the other reviews in this series cannot take: a long-run time series stretching back to 1988, and a fine-grained map of how money moves between the President’s proposal and the enacted law, both by program and across the department’s 200-plus operating units.

The reason for the different approach is a data limitation. Unlike the line departments covered elsewhere, DPWH’s budget-execution reports (SAAODB) are not available at the program/activity/project level, so the peso-by-peso execution chain cannot be traced down to individual line items. What is available, and unusually deep, is the appropriations record: a near-four-decade series of proposed, enacted, and (where sourced) obligated totals, plus a complete office-by-office breakdown of the NEP and GAA from FY 2014 to FY 2026. So this review leans into those strengths — the long arc of the department’s budget and the geography of Congressional realignment — rather than absorption ratios.

Two themes run through it. First, DPWH has grown from a roughly 6–8% slice of the national budget in the 1990s–2000s to a mid-teens share in recent years, with the 2026 GAA a sharp, anomalous contraction. Second, the budget process is a sustained tug-of-war between the executive, which concentrates the proposal in the Central Office, and Congress, which systematically redirects money toward named district engineering offices — every year on record — making the geography of those insertions, and the 2019 line-item veto that briefly reversed them, the central story.

1 Executive summary

DPWH’s footprint roughly doubled over the long run. From about 6–8% of the national budget in the 1990s–2000s, the department rose to a mid-teens share (around 17% of the GAA in 2024–2025) — evidence its expansion outpaced overall budget growth rather than merely tracking it. In real (2018-constant) terms, much of the headline post-2018 “growth” is the price level catching up; the real GAA was broadly flat between 2018 and 2023 before resuming its climb.

The 2026 GAA is a sharp anomaly. Against a proposed ₱881B (New + Automatic Appropriations), the enacted 2026 GAA falls to ₱531B — the reverse of the usual upward realignment, and a one-year collapse in DPWH’s budget share to under 8%. By program, Flood Management is realigned down by roughly ₱246B in 2026 — the single largest program cut in the series.

Congress reshapes the program mix. The Convergence and Special Support Program gains year after year, peaking at +₱265B over its proposal in 2025, while Network Development and Bridge programs are routinely trimmed from proposed levels.

The 2019 line-item veto was the largest in recent memory. Congress raised DPWH’s New Appropriations only modestly (₱544.5B proposed → ₱549.4B legislated) but heavily reallocated the money, mostly into the Local Program; the President then vetoed ₱95.4B, bringing the enacted total to ₱454.0B. The veto fell overwhelmingly on the Central Office (₱74B of the ₱95B struck).

The Unprogrammed Appropriations became a major channel. After a decade of negligible draws, UA releases to DPWH surged from ₱16B (2022) to ₱89B (2023) to ₱245B (2024) — roughly a quarter of the department’s regular budget that year — yet a large share went unobligated (about 54% utilized in 2024, 50% in 2025).

Most of the money sits centrally — but Congress moves it outward. The Central Office holds 60–75% of the enacted budget (peaking at 75% in 2025). Yet the executive’s proposal concentrates it even more, and in every year on record Congress shifts the budget away from the Central Office toward district engineering offices — a swing of nearly 30 percentage points in 2021 and 2023.

A transparent screen nominates the district offices that merit scrutiny. Scoring every office on size, intensity, persistence, and administration-tilt of its realignments, a consistent shortlist emerges — led by Batangas 4th (₱24.3B of realignments, 69% of its enacted budget, padded in all ten years), with Bulacan 1st, Sorsogon, Abra (the clearest Duterte-era office), and Metro Manila 3rd (its Marcos-era mirror) close behind.

DPWH’s budget is less a story of execution than of redistribution: a proposal lodged centrally, a Congress that reliably pushes money to named districts, and the occasional dramatic correction — a veto, a flood-control cut — that briefly reveals how the system normally works.

2 A note on the data

Why this report is different. DPWH’s SAAODB execution data is not published at the P/A/P level, so — unlike the other reviews — this one does not compute program-level absorptive capacity. It instead exploits the deep appropriations record: the long-run total series, the program breakdown, and the office-by-office NEP/GAA.

Three data layers, three time spans.

  • The long-run series (Section The long-run budget) covers FY 1988–2026 for the headline New + Automatic Appropriations, with NEP, GAA, and (where sourced) Actual Obligations. Points mark observed years; the connecting line is interpolated, so visible gaps flag where encoding is still pending.
  • The program breakdown (FY 2018–2026) uses New Appropriations only; consistent program labelling began with the 2018 budget. Program totals reconcile to the New-Appropriations column.
  • The operating-unit breakdown (FY 2014–2026) covers NEP and GAA across 200-plus offices, spanning the 16th–20th Congresses.

On the 2019 GAA. The GAA figures throughout are the enacted, post-veto levels. “Legislated (pre-veto)” reconstructs what Congress passed by adding the vetoed amounts back. Every 2019 realignment figure therefore reflects the combined effect of Congress’s reallocation and the President’s ₱95.4B veto; the veto section isolates the two.

On the Unprogrammed Appropriations (UA). The UA is a standby fund released only when revenues exceed targets or new financing is secured. Obligation data before 2013 could not be sourced; FY 2015–2017 carried no UA releases to DPWH.

On “realignment”. Throughout, realignment means the simple arithmetic difference GAA − NEP for a program or office. It is not, on its own, evidence of irregularity — but its size, persistence, and geographic concentration are what make particular offices worth a closer look. Amounts are nominal pesos unless a real (2018-constant) series is named.

3 The long-run budget, 1988–2026

The headline series is New + Automatic Appropriations — the full agency budget including automatic items — which is also the numerator for DPWH’s share of the national budget.

dpwh_newauto <- read_xlsx(data_path, sheet = "DPWH_OSec", range = "A2:D41") %>%
  rename(FY = 1, NEP = 2, GAA = 3, `Actual Obligations` = 4)
total_budget <- read_xlsx(data_path, sheet = "DPWH_OSec", range = "K2:N41") %>%
  rename(FY = 1, NEP = 2, GAA = 3, `Actual Obligations` = 4)
price_index <- read_xlsx(data_path, sheet = "DPWH_OSec", range = "U2:W41") %>%
  rename(FY = 1, CPI = 2, GDPDeflator = 3)

dpwh_long <- dpwh_newauto %>%
  pivot_longer(-FY, names_to = "Item", values_to = "Amount") %>%
  left_join(total_budget %>% pivot_longer(-FY, names_to = "Item", values_to = "TotalBudget"), by = c("FY","Item")) %>%
  left_join(price_index, by = "FY") %>%
  mutate(FY_date = as_date(str_c(FY, "-01-01")), Item = factor(Item, levels = item_levels),
         AmountB = Amount / 1e6, RealB = AmountB / (CPI / 100), Share = Amount / TotalBudget)

3.1 The nominal trend

ggplot(dpwh_long %>% filter(!is.na(AmountB))) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(data = admin_lines, aes(xintercept = Start), linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.4, alpha = 0.45) +
  geom_vline(data = congress_lines, aes(xintercept = Congress_Start), alpha = 0.10) +
  geom_text(data = admin_labels, aes(x = LabelX, y = Inf, label = President), size = 2.6, fontface = "bold", hjust = -0.05, vjust = 1.3) +
  geom_text(data = congress_labels, aes(x = LabelX, y = Inf, label = Congress), size = 2.0, color = "gray55", hjust = -0.1, vjust = 3.0) +
  geom_path(aes(FY_date, AmountB, color = Item), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(aes(FY_date, AmountB, color = Item), size = 1.2) +
  scale_x_date(date_labels = "FY '%y", breaks = congress_first_fy, minor_breaks = NULL) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +
  scale_color_manual(values = item_colors) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, NA)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "PHP billions", title = "DPWH: New + Automatic Appropriations, FY 1988–2026") +
  theme_dpwh() + theme(legend.position = c(0.15, 0.72))

In nominal terms the budget climbs steeply from the mid-2010s. Two deviations from the usual upward NEP-to-GAA pattern stand out: the 2019 GAA fell below the NEP (the year of the ₱95.4B line-item veto), and the 2026 GAA drops sharply below its NEP — the reverse of the realignment seen in most other years.

3.2 Share of the national budget

ggplot(dpwh_long %>% filter(!is.na(Share))) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(data = admin_lines, aes(xintercept = Start), linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.4, alpha = 0.45) +
  geom_vline(data = congress_lines, aes(xintercept = Congress_Start), alpha = 0.10) +
  geom_text(data = admin_labels, aes(x = LabelX, y = Inf, label = President), size = 2.6, fontface = "bold", hjust = -0.05, vjust = 1.3) +
  geom_text(data = congress_labels, aes(x = LabelX, y = Inf, label = Congress), size = 2.0, color = "gray55", hjust = -0.1, vjust = 3.0) +
  geom_path(aes(FY_date, Share, color = Item), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(aes(FY_date, Share, color = Item), size = 1.2) +
  scale_x_date(date_labels = "FY '%y", breaks = congress_first_fy, minor_breaks = NULL) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1), expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +
  scale_color_manual(values = item_colors) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, NA)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "% of total appropriations", title = "DPWH as a share of the national budget, FY 1988–2026") +
  theme_dpwh() + theme(legend.position = c(0.13, 0.6))

Rescaling the same money against the size of the whole budget shows the department’s footprint roughly doubling — from ~6–8% in the 1990s–2000s to a mid-teens share in recent years. DPWH’s expansion outpaced overall budget growth, rather than merely tracking it. The 2026 NEP-to-GAA gap reappears here as a one-year contraction in the GAA share to under 8%.

3.3 In real terms (constant 2018 prices)

ggplot(dpwh_long %>% filter(!is.na(RealB))) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(data = admin_lines, aes(xintercept = Start), linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.4, alpha = 0.45) +
  geom_vline(data = congress_lines, aes(xintercept = Congress_Start), alpha = 0.10) +
  geom_text(data = admin_labels, aes(x = LabelX, y = Inf, label = President), size = 2.6, fontface = "bold", hjust = -0.05, vjust = 1.3) +
  geom_text(data = congress_labels, aes(x = LabelX, y = Inf, label = Congress), size = 2.0, color = "gray55", hjust = -0.1, vjust = 3.0) +
  geom_path(aes(FY_date, RealB, color = Item), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(aes(FY_date, RealB, color = Item), size = 1.2) +
  scale_x_date(date_labels = "FY '%y", breaks = congress_first_fy, minor_breaks = NULL) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +
  scale_color_manual(values = item_colors) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, NA)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "PHP billions (constant 2018 prices)",
       title = "DPWH in real terms, FY 1988–2026", subtitle = "Deflated by the CPI (2018 = 100)") +
  theme_dpwh() + theme(legend.position = c(0.15, 0.72))

Holding prices constant at 2018 tempers the nominal story: much of the post-2018 “growth” is the price level catching up. In real terms the GAA is broadly flat between 2018 and 2023 before resuming its climb — a useful counterweight to headline peso figures.

Summary table (interactive)

dpwh_tbl <- dpwh_long %>%
  mutate(code = recode(Item, "NEP"="NEP","GAA"="GAA","Actual Obligations"="AO")) %>%
  select(FY, code, AmountB, RealB, Share) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = code, values_from = c(AmountB, RealB, Share)) %>%
  transmute(FY, `NEP (B)` = round(AmountB_NEP,1), `GAA (B)` = round(AmountB_GAA,1),
            `Actual Oblig. (B)` = round(AmountB_AO,1),
            `Realignment GAA−NEP (B)` = round(AmountB_GAA - AmountB_NEP,1),
            `DPWH share of GAA` = Share_GAA, `GAA, real 2018 (B)` = round(RealB_GAA,1)) %>%
  arrange(desc(FY))
dt_table(dpwh_tbl, page = 12,
         money_cols = c("NEP (B)","GAA (B)","Actual Oblig. (B)","Realignment GAA−NEP (B)","GAA, real 2018 (B)"),
         pct_cols = "DPWH share of GAA")

4 The budget by program, 2018–2026

DPWH organises its budget into eight programs. These figures are New Appropriations, rescaled to PHP billions.

byprogram <- read_xlsx(data_path, sheet = "NewAppro_ByProgram") %>%
  mutate(Vetoed = replace_na(as.numeric(Vetoed), 0), Program = factor(Program, levels = dpwh_programs),
         NEP_B = NEP/1e9, GAA_B = GAA/1e9, Veto_B = Vetoed/1e9, Realign = (GAA - NEP)/1e9)
substantive <- setdiff(dpwh_programs, c("General Administration and Support","Support to Operations"))

4.1 Composition over time

ggplot(byprogram, aes(FY, GAA_B, fill = Program)) +
  geom_col(color = "black", linewidth = 0.2, width = 0.82) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 2022.5, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.45) +
  annotate("text", x = 2020, y = Inf, label = "Duterte", vjust = 1.4, size = 3, fontface = "bold") +
  annotate("text", x = 2024.5, y = Inf, label = "Marcos Jr.", vjust = 1.4, size = 3, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2018:2026) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.07))) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = program_colors, labels = function(x) str_wrap(x, 26)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "PHP billions", title = "DPWH budget composition by program (GAA)") +
  guides(fill = guide_legend(ncol = 2)) +
  theme_dpwh() + theme(legend.text = element_text(size = 8), legend.key.size = unit(0.4, "cm"))

ggplot(byprogram, aes(FY, GAA_B, fill = Program)) +
  geom_col(color = "black", linewidth = 0.2, width = 0.82, position = position_fill()) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 2022.5, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.45) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2018:2026) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.04))) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = program_colors, labels = function(x) str_wrap(x, 26)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Share of DPWH GAA", title = "DPWH budget composition by program (100% stacked)") +
  guides(fill = guide_legend(ncol = 2)) +
  theme_dpwh() + theme(legend.text = element_text(size = 8), legend.key.size = unit(0.4, "cm"))

The composition shift is stark: the Convergence and Special Support Program (salmon) swells under the current administration, while the Network Development and Flood Management shares contract — most dramatically in the 2026 GAA, where Flood Management is cut to a sliver. The dashed line marks the Duterte-to-Marcos transition (FY 2023 is the first budget proposed by the current administration).

comp_tbl <- byprogram %>% transmute(FY, Program, GAA_B = round(GAA_B,1)) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = FY, values_from = GAA_B) %>% arrange(factor(Program, levels = dpwh_programs))
dt_table(comp_tbl %>% rename(`Program — GAA (B)` = Program), page = 8)

4.2 NEP vs GAA, and the realignments

nepvgaa <- byprogram %>% filter(Program %in% substantive) %>%
  mutate(Program = factor(Program, levels = substantive)) %>%
  select(FY, Program, NEP_B, GAA_B) %>%
  pivot_longer(c(NEP_B, GAA_B), names_to = "Item", values_to = "AmountB") %>%
  mutate(Item = factor(if_else(Item == "NEP_B", "NEP", "GAA"), levels = c("NEP","GAA")))
nepvgaa_var <- byprogram %>% filter(Program %in% substantive) %>%
  mutate(Program = factor(Program, levels = substantive), ypos = pmax(NEP_B, GAA_B) * 1.05,
         lab = if_else(Realign >= 0, str_c("+", format(round(Realign,1), nsmall = 1)), format(round(Realign,1), nsmall = 1)))
ggplot(nepvgaa) +
  geom_col(aes(FY, AmountB, fill = Item), position = position_dodge(width = 0.75), width = 0.66) +
  geom_text(data = nepvgaa_var, aes(FY, ypos, label = lab), size = 2.3) +
  facet_wrap(~Program, scales = "free_y", ncol = 3, labeller = label_wrap_gen(24)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(2018, 2020, 2022, 2024, 2026)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.13))) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("NEP" = "#ffa6b0", "GAA" = "#3994fc")) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "PHP billions",
       title = "By program: NEP vs GAA, with net realignments (GAA − NEP)",
       subtitle = "Labels show the net change from NEP to enacted GAA (PHP B)") +
  theme_dpwh(grid_x = FALSE) + theme(strip.text = element_text(size = 8))

The same realignments read most cleanly as a matrix — each cell is the net move from the President’s proposal to the enacted law, with blue = increases and red = cuts:

ggplot(byprogram, aes(FY, fct_rev(Program), fill = Realign)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(Realign)), size = 2.4) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2018:2026, position = "top", expand = expansion(mult = 0)) +
  scale_y_discrete(labels = function(x) str_wrap(x, 28)) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#ffa6b0", mid = "white", high = "#3994fc", midpoint = 0, labels = comma, name = "GAA − NEP\n(PHP B)") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL, title = "Net realignments from NEP to GAA, by program",
       caption = "2019 nets out the ₱95.4B presidential veto (see the veto section).") +
  theme_dpwh() + theme(panel.grid = element_blank(), axis.text.y = element_text(size = 9),
        legend.position = "right", legend.key.width = unit(0.4, "cm"))

Two patterns stand out: the Convergence and Special Support Program gains year after year (peaking at +₱265B in 2025), while Network Development and Bridge are routinely trimmed from their proposed levels. The 2026 column is exceptional — Flood Management is realigned down by ~₱246B, the largest single-program cut in the series.

realign_tbl <- byprogram %>% transmute(FY, Program, Realign = round(Realign,1)) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = FY, values_from = Realign) %>% arrange(factor(Program, levels = dpwh_programs))
dt_table(realign_tbl %>% rename(`Program — realignment (B)` = Program), page = 8)

5 The 2019 line-item veto

In FY 2019, Congress raised DPWH’s New Appropriations only modestly in aggregate (from a ₱544.5B proposal to a ₱549.4B legislated total) but heavily reallocated the money — above all into the Local Program. The President then exercised line-item vetoes worth ₱95.4B, almost all from that program, bringing the enacted GAA down to ₱454.0B.

veto_total <- tibble(
  Stage = factor(c("Proposed\n(NEP)","Legislated\n(pre-veto)","Enacted\n(post-veto)"),
                 levels = c("Proposed\n(NEP)","Legislated\n(pre-veto)","Enacted\n(post-veto)")),
  AmountB = c(sum(byprogram$NEP_B[byprogram$FY == 2019]),
              sum(byprogram$GAA_B[byprogram$FY == 2019]) + sum(byprogram$Veto_B[byprogram$FY == 2019]),
              sum(byprogram$GAA_B[byprogram$FY == 2019])))
ggplot(veto_total, aes(Stage, AmountB, fill = Stage)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.62) +
  geom_text(aes(label = comma(AmountB, accuracy = 0.1)), vjust = -0.5, size = 3.4) +
  annotate("segment", x = 2, xend = 3, y = 575, yend = 575) +
  annotate("text", x = 2.5, y = 590, label = "vetoed: −₱95.4B", size = 3.2, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.18))) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#ffa6b0","#bcbef2","#3994fc")) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "PHP billions", title = "FY 2019: proposed → legislated → enacted (after the veto)") +
  theme_dpwh() + theme(legend.position = "none")

veto_prog <- byprogram %>% filter(FY == 2019) %>%
  transmute(Program, `Proposed (NEP)` = NEP_B, `Legislated (pre-veto)` = GAA_B + Veto_B, `Enacted (post-veto)` = GAA_B) %>%
  pivot_longer(-Program, names_to = "Stage", values_to = "AmountB") %>%
  mutate(Stage = factor(Stage, levels = c("Proposed (NEP)","Legislated (pre-veto)","Enacted (post-veto)")),
         Program = factor(Program, levels = dpwh_programs))
ggplot(veto_prog, aes(Stage, AmountB, fill = Stage)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7) +
  facet_wrap(~Program, scales = "free_y", ncol = 4, labeller = label_wrap_gen(22)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1))) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Proposed (NEP)" = "#ffa6b0","Legislated (pre-veto)" = "#bcbef2","Enacted (post-veto)" = "#3994fc")) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "PHP billions", title = "FY 2019 by program: proposed, legislated, and enacted after the veto") +
  theme_dpwh() + theme(strip.text = element_text(size = 8), axis.text.x = element_blank())

The veto was overwhelmingly a Local Program event: only that program (plus token overhead amounts) shows a legislated bar towering over the enacted level. Unpacking the vetoed line items by type of work and by engineering office shows where the realigned money had been parked.

veto_detail <- read_xlsx(data_path, sheet = "2019_Veto") %>% mutate(Veto_B = GAA_Veto / 1e9)
veto_detail %>% group_by(Activity) %>% summarize(Veto_B = sum(Veto_B), .groups = "drop") %>%
  ggplot(aes(Veto_B, fct_reorder(Activity, Veto_B))) +
  geom_col(fill = "#3994fc") +
  geom_text(aes(label = format(round(Veto_B,1), nsmall = 1)), hjust = -0.15, size = 3) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.12))) +
  labs(x = "PHP billions vetoed", y = NULL, title = "What the 2019 veto struck, by type of work") +
  theme_dpwh() + theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(), axis.text.y = element_text(size = 9.5))

veto_detail %>% group_by(Office) %>% summarize(Veto_B = sum(Veto_B), .groups = "drop") %>%
  slice_max(Veto_B, n = 15) %>%
  ggplot(aes(Veto_B, fct_reorder(Office, Veto_B))) +
  geom_col(fill = "#ffa6b0") +
  geom_text(aes(label = format(round(Veto_B,2), nsmall = 2)), hjust = -0.15, size = 2.8) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.14))) +
  labs(x = "PHP billions vetoed", y = NULL, title = "Where the 2019 veto struck: top 15 offices",
       subtitle = "The DPWH Central Office alone accounts for ~₱74B of the ₱95.4B vetoed") +
  theme_dpwh() + theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(), axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8.5))

byprogram %>% filter(FY == 2019) %>%
  transmute(Program, `Proposed (NEP)` = NEP_B, `Legislated (pre-veto)` = GAA_B + Veto_B, Vetoed = Veto_B, `Enacted (post-veto)` = GAA_B) %>%
  bind_rows(tibble(Program = "DPWH TOTAL",
                   `Proposed (NEP)` = sum(byprogram$NEP_B[byprogram$FY == 2019]),
                   `Legislated (pre-veto)` = sum((byprogram$GAA_B + byprogram$Veto_B)[byprogram$FY == 2019]),
                   Vetoed = sum(byprogram$Veto_B[byprogram$FY == 2019]),
                   `Enacted (post-veto)` = sum(byprogram$GAA_B[byprogram$FY == 2019]))) %>%
  mutate(across(-Program, ~comma(.x, accuracy = 0.1))) %>%
  kbl_clean(font = 12.5, align = c("l", rep("r", 4)),
            caption = "FY 2019 DPWH: proposed, legislated (pre-veto), vetoed, and enacted (post-veto) — PHP billions") %>%
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE)
FY 2019 DPWH: proposed, legislated (pre-veto), vetoed, and enacted (post-veto) — PHP billions
Program Proposed (NEP) Legislated (pre-veto) Vetoed Enacted (post-veto)
General Administration and Support 10.5 12.9 0.3 12.6
Support to Operations 50.0 31.9 0.5 31.5
Asset Preservation Program 59.0 45.5 0.0 45.5
Network Development Program 122.9 95.3 0.0 95.3
Bridge Program 33.7 24.3 0.0 24.3
Flood Management Program 114.3 90.7 0.0 90.7
Local Program 119.9 218.4 94.6 123.9
Convergence and Special Support Program 34.3 30.3 0.0 30.3
DPWH TOTAL 544.5 549.4 95.4 454.0

6 The Unprogrammed Appropriations

The Unprogrammed Appropriations (UA) is a standby fund released only when revenue collections exceed targets or new financing is secured. Because releases happen outside the regular program structure, they are harder to track — and DPWH’s draw on the UA has become a focus of scrutiny, as a number of flood-control projects later flagged as questionable were reportedly financed through this route.

ua <- read_xlsx(data_path, sheet = "DPWH_UA") %>%
  mutate(Allotments_B = Allotments/1e6, Obligations_B = Obligations/1e6)
ua_long <- ua %>% select(FY, Allotments_B, Obligations_B) %>%
  pivot_longer(-FY, names_to = "Item", values_to = "AmountB") %>%
  mutate(Item = recode(Item, Allotments_B = "Allotments", Obligations_B = "Obligations"),
         Item = factor(Item, levels = c("Allotments","Obligations")))
ggplot(ua_long %>% filter(!is.na(AmountB)), aes(FY, AmountB, fill = Item)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.7), width = 0.66) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2011:2025) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.06))) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Allotments" = "#ffa6b0", "Obligations" = "#3994fc")) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "PHP billions", title = "DPWH releases from the Unprogrammed Appropriations",
       subtitle = "Allotments released and obligations incurred, by fiscal year") +
  theme_dpwh() + theme(legend.position = c(0.16, 0.78))

After a decade of negligible draws, UA releases to DPWH surged from ₱16B in 2022 to ₱89B in 2023 and ₱245B in 2024 — the latter alone roughly a quarter of the department’s entire regular budget that year. Yet a large share of these allotments went unobligated (about 54% utilized in 2024, 50% in 2025) — exactly the kind of pattern that merits questioning at the hearings.

ua %>% transmute(FY, `Allotments (B)` = round(Allotments_B,2), `Obligations (B)` = round(Obligations_B,2),
                 Utilization = Obligations_B/Allotments_B) %>%
  mutate(`Allotments (B)` = comma(`Allotments (B)`, accuracy = 0.01),
         `Obligations (B)` = ifelse(is.na(`Obligations (B)`), "\u2013", comma(`Obligations (B)`, accuracy = 0.01)),
         Utilization = ifelse(is.na(Utilization)|is.nan(Utilization)|is.infinite(Utilization), "\u2013", percent(Utilization, accuracy = 1))) %>%
  kbl_clean(font = 12.5, align = "lrrr",
            caption = "DPWH releases from the Unprogrammed Appropriations, PHP billions")
DPWH releases from the Unprogrammed Appropriations, PHP billions
FY Allotments (B) Obligations (B) Utilization
2011 0.81 – –
2012 0.02 – –
2013 0.00 0.00 –
2014 0.04 0.04 99%
2015 0.00 0.00 –
2016 0.00 0.00 –
2017 0.00 0.00 –
2018 11.45 7.63 67%
2019 0.01 0.01 100%
2020 1.84 1.80 97%
2021 1.36 1.31 96%
2022 16.26 15.11 93%
2023 89.33 51.72 58%
2024 244.74 132.19 54%
2025 59.08 29.62 50%

7 New appropriations by operating unit

DPWH disburses its budget across some 200-plus operating units — the Central Office, regional offices, and district engineering offices (DEOs). The office-by-office data run from the FY 2014 budget through FY 2026, spanning the 16th–20th Congresses, three administrations, and seven House Speakers. The FY 2019 GAA here reflects the budget after the ₱95.4B veto.

byoperunit <- read_xlsx(data_path, sheet = "NewAppro_ByOperUnit") %>%
  mutate(FY = as.integer(FY), GAA = replace_na(GAA, 0), NEP = replace_na(NEP, 0),
         GAA_B = GAA/1e9, NEP_B = NEP/1e9, Realign = (GAA - NEP)/1e9,
         Congress = case_when(FY %in% 2014:2016 ~ "16th (Aquino)", FY %in% 2017:2019 ~ "17th (Duterte)",
                              FY %in% 2020:2022 ~ "18th (Duterte)", FY %in% 2023:2025 ~ "19th (Marcos)", FY == 2026 ~ "20th (Marcos)"),
         Admin = case_when(FY %in% 2014:2016 ~ "Aquino", FY %in% 2017:2022 ~ "Duterte", FY %in% 2023:2026 ~ "Marcos"))
deo <- byoperunit %>% filter(str_detect(Office, "D.E. Office"), FY >= 2017)
n_years_deo <- dplyr::n_distinct(deo$FY)

Because realignments toward district offices are made on the House floor, the relevant political actor is often the Speaker of the day as much as the sitting President.

tibble::tribble(
  ~Speaker, ~`Term began`, ~Congress, ~`FY budgets`, ~Administration,
  "Feliciano Belmonte Jr.","22 Jul 2013","16th","FY2014–2016","Aquino",
  "Pantaleon Alvarez","25 Jul 2016","17th","FY2017–2018","Duterte",
  "Gloria Macapagal Arroyo","23 Jul 2018","17th","FY2019*","Duterte",
  "Alan Peter Cayetano","22 Jul 2019","18th","FY2020","Duterte",
  "Lord Allan Velasco","12 Oct 2020","18th","FY2021–2022","Duterte",
  "Martin Romualdez","25 Jul 2022","19th","FY2023–2025","Marcos",
  'Faustino "Bojie" Dy III',"17 Sep 2025","20th","FY2026","Marcos") %>%
  kbl_clean(font = 12.5, align = "lllll",
            caption = "House Speakers presiding over the DPWH budgets analysed here. *FY2019's encoded GAA is post-veto.")
House Speakers presiding over the DPWH budgets analysed here. *FY2019’s encoded GAA is post-veto.
Speaker Term began Congress FY budgets Administration
Feliciano Belmonte Jr.  22 Jul 2013 16th FY2014–2016 Aquino
Pantaleon Alvarez 25 Jul 2016 17th FY2017–2018 Duterte
Gloria Macapagal Arroyo 23 Jul 2018 17th FY2019* Duterte
Alan Peter Cayetano 22 Jul 2019 18th FY2020 Duterte
Lord Allan Velasco 12 Oct 2020 18th FY2021–2022 Duterte
Martin Romualdez 25 Jul 2022 19th FY2023–2025 Marcos
Faustino “Bojie” Dy III 17 Sep 2025 20th FY2026 Marcos

7.1 Most of the money sits in the Central Office

central_split <- byoperunit %>%
  mutate(Unit = if_else(Office == "Central Office", "Central Office", "All Other Offices")) %>%
  group_by(FY, Unit) %>% summarize(GAA_B = sum(GAA_B), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(Unit = factor(Unit, levels = c("All Other Offices","Central Office")))
central_lab <- central_split %>% group_by(FY) %>%
  summarize(tot = sum(GAA_B), central = sum(GAA_B[Unit == "Central Office"]), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(lab = percent(central/tot, accuracy = 1))
ggplot(central_split, aes(FY, GAA_B, fill = Unit)) +
  geom_col(color = "black", linewidth = 0.2, width = 0.78) +
  geom_text(data = central_lab, aes(FY, tot, label = lab), inherit.aes = FALSE, vjust = -0.5, size = 3) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(2014, 2017, 2020, 2023, 2026)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.09))) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("All Other Offices" = "#bcbef2", "Central Office" = "#3994fc")) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "GAA, PHP billions", title = "Most of the DPWH budget is lodged in the Central Office",
       subtitle = "Labels show the Central Office's share of the GAA") +
  theme_dpwh()

Across the series the Central Office holds most of the enacted budget — typically 60–75%, peaking at 75% in 2025 — with 2015–2016 (both under 50%) and the unusual 2026 GAA (62%) the main exceptions. A single operating unit carrying most of the department’s budget is a reason for closer scrutiny, not less. The enacted shares also understate how concentrated the executive’s proposal is — the subject of the next section.

central_split %>% pivot_wider(names_from = Unit, values_from = GAA_B) %>%
  mutate(Total = `Central Office` + `All Other Offices`, `Central share` = `Central Office`/Total) %>%
  transmute(FY, `Central Office (B)` = round(`Central Office`,1), `All Other Offices (B)` = round(`All Other Offices`,1),
            `Total (B)` = round(Total,1), `Central share`) %>%
  dt_table(page = 13, money_cols = c("Central Office (B)","All Other Offices (B)","Total (B)"), pct_cols = "Central share")

7.2 From proposal to law: Central Office to the districts

The executive’s proposal concentrates the budget centrally even more than the enacted law does. In every year on record, Congress channels tens — and in recent years hundreds — of billions toward district engineering offices between the proposal and the enacted law, while cutting the Central Office in most.

byoperunit %>%
  mutate(Unit = case_when(Office == "Central Office" ~ "Central Office",
                          str_detect(Office, "D.E. Office") ~ "District Engineering Offices", TRUE ~ "Other")) %>%
  filter(Unit != "Other") %>%
  group_by(FY, Unit) %>% summarize(Shift_B = sum(GAA_B) - sum(NEP_B), .groups = "drop") %>%
  ggplot(aes(factor(FY), Shift_B, fill = Unit)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.72), width = 0.66, color = "black", linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linewidth = 0.4) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Central Office" = "#3994fc", "District Engineering Offices" = "#bcbef2")) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Change in budget, NEP → GAA (PHP B)",
       title = "Congress redirects the budget toward the district engineering offices",
       subtitle = "District offices gain every year; the Central Office is cut in most") +
  theme_dpwh()

byoperunit %>%
  mutate(Unit = case_when(Office == "Central Office" ~ "Central Office",
                          str_detect(Office, "D.E. Office") ~ "District Engineering Offices", TRUE ~ "Other")) %>%
  group_by(FY, Unit) %>% summarize(NEP_B = sum(NEP_B), GAA_B = sum(GAA_B), .groups = "drop") %>%
  group_by(FY) %>% mutate(`Proposed (NEP)` = NEP_B/sum(NEP_B), `Enacted (GAA)` = GAA_B/sum(GAA_B)) %>% ungroup() %>%
  filter(Unit != "Other") %>%
  pivot_longer(c(`Proposed (NEP)`,`Enacted (GAA)`), names_to = "Stage", values_to = "Share") %>%
  mutate(Stage = factor(Stage, levels = c("Proposed (NEP)","Enacted (GAA)"))) %>%
  ggplot(aes(factor(FY), Share, fill = Stage)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.72), width = 0.66, color = "black", linewidth = 0.2) +
  facet_wrap(~Unit) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.06))) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Proposed (NEP)" = "#ffa6b0", "Enacted (GAA)" = "#3994fc")) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Share of new appropriations", title = "Share of new appropriations: proposed vs enacted",
       subtitle = "The Central Office is proposed a larger share than enacted; for district offices it is the reverse") +
  theme_dpwh() + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(size = 7.5, angle = 90, vjust = 0.5))

In share terms the pattern is cleaner still: in every year (2014–2026) the Central Office’s share falls from NEP to GAA while the districts’ share rises — a swing reaching nearly 30 percentage points in 2021 and 2023, when the proposal parked almost the entire budget centrally.

Two of those Central cuts come with an asterisk. The operating-unit GAA encoded for 2019 is the budget after the President’s ₱95.4B veto, and that veto landed overwhelmingly on the Central Office (₱74B of the ₱95B struck, versus ₱21B across districts). Congress’s own pre-veto budget was routine — it trimmed the Central Office by ~₱26B and added ~₱30B to the districts. Because the veto hit the Central Office hardest, it paradoxically raised the district share of the final budget.

veto_unit <- byoperunit %>% filter(FY == 2019) %>%
  mutate(Unit = case_when(Office == "Central Office" ~ "Central Office",
                          str_detect(Office, "D.E. Office") ~ "District Engineering Offices", TRUE ~ "Other")) %>%
  group_by(Unit) %>% summarize(NEP_B = sum(NEP_B), Enacted_B = sum(GAA_B), .groups = "drop") %>%
  left_join(read_xlsx(data_path, sheet = "2019_Veto") %>%
              mutate(Unit = case_when(Office == "Central Office" ~ "Central Office",
                                      str_detect(Office, "D.E. Office") ~ "District Engineering Offices", TRUE ~ "Other")) %>%
              group_by(Unit) %>% summarize(Vetoed_B = sum(GAA_Veto)/1e9, .groups = "drop"), by = "Unit") %>%
  filter(Unit != "Other") %>% mutate(Vetoed_B = replace_na(Vetoed_B, 0))
veto_unit %>% select(Unit, `Enacted (survived)` = Enacted_B, Vetoed = Vetoed_B) %>%
  pivot_longer(c(`Enacted (survived)`, Vetoed), names_to = "Stage", values_to = "Amount_B") %>%
  mutate(Stage = factor(Stage, levels = c("Vetoed","Enacted (survived)"))) %>%
  ggplot(aes(Unit, Amount_B, fill = Stage)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6, color = "black", linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_point(data = veto_unit, aes(Unit, NEP_B), inherit.aes = FALSE, shape = 18, size = 4, color = "#ffa6b0") +
  geom_text(data = veto_unit, aes(Unit, Enacted_B + Vetoed_B/2, label = paste0("vetoed\n", round(Vetoed_B), "B")),
            inherit.aes = FALSE, size = 2.8, color = "grey25", lineheight = 0.9) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1))) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Enacted (survived)" = "#3994fc", "Vetoed" = "grey70")) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "PHP billions", title = "The 2019 line-item veto, by operating unit",
       subtitle = "Bars show what Congress passed; grey is the part the President struck. Diamonds mark the proposal (NEP).") +
  theme_dpwh()

7.3 Top district engineering offices, by size and realignment

Setting the Central Office aside: which district offices command the largest budgets, and how much of each was in the proposal versus added on enactment? The bars split the largest offices into what was proposed (grey) and what Congress added (purple).

pva <- deo %>% group_by(Office) %>%
  summarize(`In proposal (NEP)` = sum(NEP_B), `Added by Congress` = sum(Realign), GAA_B = sum(GAA_B), .groups = "drop") %>%
  slice_max(GAA_B, n = 15)
pva %>% pivot_longer(c(`In proposal (NEP)`, `Added by Congress`), names_to = "Component", values_to = "AmountB") %>%
  mutate(Component = factor(Component, levels = c("In proposal (NEP)","Added by Congress")),
         Office = factor(Office, levels = arrange(pva, GAA_B)$Office)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(AmountB, Office, fill = Component)) +
  geom_col(color = "black", linewidth = 0.2) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("In proposal (NEP)" = "grey75", "Added by Congress" = "#9a82c9")) +
  labs(x = "PHP billions, cumulative FY2017–2026", y = NULL,
       title = "District engineering offices with the largest enacted budgets",
       subtitle = "Grey = executive proposal (NEP); purple = added by Congress on enactment") +
  theme_dpwh() + theme(legend.position = c(0.8, 0.3), panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(), axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8.5))

Splitting those Congressional additions by administration shows which offices were favoured by whom — Abra’s windfalls came overwhelmingly under Duterte, while Bulacan 1st and Tarlac lean Marcos.

pva_rank <- deo %>% filter(FY != 2026) %>% group_by(Office) %>% summarize(GAA_B = sum(GAA_B), .groups = "drop") %>% slice_max(GAA_B, n = 15)
pva_seg <- bind_rows(
  deo %>% filter(FY != 2026) %>% group_by(Office) %>% summarize(AmountB = sum(NEP_B), .groups = "drop") %>% mutate(Component = "In proposal (NEP)"),
  deo %>% filter(FY != 2026) %>% group_by(Office, Admin) %>% summarize(AmountB = sum(Realign), .groups = "drop") %>%
    transmute(Office, Component = str_c("Added under ", Admin), AmountB))
pva_seg %>% semi_join(pva_rank, by = "Office") %>%
  mutate(Office = factor(Office, levels = arrange(pva_rank, GAA_B)$Office),
         Component = factor(Component, levels = c("In proposal (NEP)","Added under Duterte","Added under Marcos"))) %>%
  ggplot(aes(AmountB, Office, fill = Component)) +
  geom_col(color = "black", linewidth = 0.2) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("In proposal (NEP)" = "grey75","Added under Duterte" = "#e15759","Added under Marcos" = "#3994fc")) +
  labs(x = "PHP billions, cumulative FY2017–2025", y = NULL,
       title = "Proposed budget and what each administration added",
       subtitle = "FY2026 omitted: the one year Congress cut, rather than added to, district budgets") +
  theme_dpwh() + theme(legend.position = c(0.74, 0.26), panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(), axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8.5))

The realignment matrix below splits each favoured office’s realignments between the two administrations.

top15 <- deo %>% group_by(Office) %>% summarize(tot = sum(Realign), .groups = "drop") %>% slice_max(tot, n = 15) %>% pull(Office)
deo %>% filter(Office %in% top15) %>% mutate(Office = factor(Office, levels = rev(top15))) %>%
  ggplot(aes(FY, Office, fill = Realign)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_text(aes(label = format(round(Realign,1), nsmall = 1)), size = 2.2) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2017:2026, position = "top", expand = expansion(mult = 0)) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#ffa6b0", mid = "white", high = "#3994fc", midpoint = 0, labels = comma, name = "GAA − NEP\n(PHP B)") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL, title = "Net realignments by district engineering office and year") +
  theme_dpwh() + theme(panel.grid = element_blank(), axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8.5),
        legend.position = "right", legend.key.width = unit(0.4, "cm"))

Top district offices by total realignment (interactive)

deo %>% group_by(Office, Admin) %>% summarize(Realign = sum(Realign), .groups = "drop") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = Admin, values_from = Realign, values_fill = 0) %>%
  mutate(Total = Duterte + Marcos) %>% arrange(desc(Total)) %>%
  transmute(Office, `Duterte (B)` = round(Duterte,1), `Marcos (B)` = round(Marcos,1), `Total (B)` = round(Total,1)) %>%
  dt_table(page = 15, money_cols = c("Duterte (B)","Marcos (B)","Total (B)"))

7.4 Which district offices merit a closer look?

Rather than single out any region, this applies a transparent screen to every district office. Each is scored on four independent signals over FY 2017–2026: size (top-ten recipient of realignments), intensity (realignments ≥ 60% of its enacted budget), persistence (a realignment in at least eight of ten years), and tilt (Duterte- vs Marcos-era realignment totals differ by at least ₱4B). Offices that trip several signals at once are the natural candidates for scrutiny.

screen_base <- deo %>% group_by(Office) %>%
  summarize(NEP_B = sum(NEP_B), GAA_B = sum(GAA_B), Insertion = sum(Realign),
            InsShare = sum(Realign)/sum(GAA_B), YearsInserted = sum(Realign > 0.5), .groups = "drop") %>%
  left_join(deo %>% group_by(Office, Admin) %>% summarize(r = sum(Realign), .groups = "drop") %>%
              pivot_wider(names_from = Admin, values_from = r, values_fill = 0) %>%
              transmute(Office, Duterte, Marcos, Tilt = Duterte - Marcos), by = "Office") %>%
  mutate(across(c(Duterte, Marcos, Tilt), ~replace_na(.x, 0)))
screen_nonep <- screen_base %>% filter(NEP_B == 0) %>% arrange(desc(GAA_B))
deo_screen <- screen_base %>% filter(NEP_B > 0) %>%
  mutate(flag_big = min_rank(desc(Insertion)) <= 10, flag_intense = InsShare >= 0.60,
         flag_persistent = YearsInserted >= n_years_deo - 2, flag_tilted = abs(Tilt) >= 4,
         nflags = flag_big + flag_intense + flag_persistent + flag_tilted,
         Signals = str_c(if_else(flag_big, "large; ", ""), if_else(flag_intense, "realignment-heavy; ", ""),
                         if_else(flag_persistent, "every year; ", ""),
                         if_else(flag_tilted & Tilt > 0, "Duterte-leaning; ", ""),
                         if_else(flag_tilted & Tilt < 0, "Marcos-leaning; ", "")),
         Signals = str_remove(Signals, "; $"))
screen_offscale <- deo_screen %>% filter(InsShare < 0) %>% arrange(InsShare)
nonep_note <- if (nrow(screen_nonep) == 0) "none" else str_c(screen_nonep$Office, collapse = "; ")
ggplot(deo_screen, aes(Insertion, InsShare)) +
  geom_point(aes(size = YearsInserted, fill = Tilt), shape = 21, color = "black", alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_text_repel(data = deo_screen %>% filter(nflags >= 2 | Insertion >= 9), aes(label = Office),
                  size = 2.6, max.overlaps = 30, segment.alpha = 0.4) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#3994fc", mid = "grey88", high = "#ff5d6c", midpoint = 0, labels = comma,
                       name = "Admin tilt, PHP B\n(red = Duterte,\nblue = Marcos)") +
  scale_size(range = c(1, 6), name = "Years with a\nrealignment") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = comma) + scale_y_continuous(labels = percent) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, NA)) +
  labs(x = "Total realignment, FY2017–2026 (PHP billions)", y = "Realignments as a share of the office's enacted budget",
       title = "Screening district engineering offices: size and intensity of realignments",
       subtitle = "Top-right = both large and realignment-heavy; colour shows which administration leaned in") +
  theme_dpwh(grid_x = TRUE) +
  theme(legend.position = "right", legend.title = element_text(size = 8), legend.text = element_text(size = 8))

1 newly-created district offices had no proposed budget (NEP = 0) anywhere in the window — money Congress provided where the executive proposed nothing — and are excluded from the scatter: Cavite 5th D.E. Office.

The screen returns a consistent shortlist — none of it about a single region. Batangas 4th D.E. Office is the largest single recipient of realignments — ₱24.3B, or 69% of its enacted budget, padded in all ten years and favoured fairly evenly by both administrations (₱14.5B Duterte, ₱9.7B Marcos), a standing fixture regardless of who holds power. Sorsogon is similarly relentless (₱16.0B, 66%, every year) but tilts Marcos-ward. Bulacan 1st (₱20.7B) leans Marcos. At the politically lopsided extremes sit Abra — the clearest Duterte-era office (₱13.2B vs ₱3.1B) — and Metro Manila 3rd, its Marcos-era mirror (₱10.7B vs ₱3.8B). These offices, not any one province, are where the strongest patterns lie and where project-level records would be worth pulling.

deo_screen %>% arrange(desc(nflags), desc(Insertion)) %>% slice_head(n = 15) %>%
  transmute(Office, `Realignment (B)` = round(Insertion,1), `Realignment share` = InsShare,
            `Years realigned` = str_c(YearsInserted, "/", n_years_deo),
            `Duterte (B)` = round(Duterte,1), `Marcos (B)` = round(Marcos,1), Flags = nflags, Signals) %>%
  dt_table(page = 15, money_cols = c("Realignment (B)","Duterte (B)","Marcos (B)"), pct_cols = "Realignment share")

8 Questions for discussion

  1. The 2026 anomaly. The enacted 2026 GAA collapses to roughly 60% of its own proposal, with Flood Management realigned down ~₱246B — a reversal of every prior year’s pattern. What drove this, and is it a one-year correction or a structural shift away from flood-control spending?
  2. The Convergence program’s rise. The Convergence and Special Support Program is augmented year after year, peaking at +₱265B over its proposal in 2025. What does this catch-all program fund, and why does Congress consistently grow it beyond the executive’s request?
  3. The Unprogrammed Appropriations channel. UA releases to DPWH leapt to ₱245B in 2024 at ~54% utilization. Given that flagged flood-control projects were reportedly financed this way, what controls govern UA releases, and why does so much go unobligated?
  4. Central concentration vs district redistribution. The executive proposes most of the budget centrally; Congress reliably moves it to named district offices every year. Which is the more accountable structure — and how are centrally-managed projects, which hold the majority of the budget, being scrutinized?
  5. The flagged district offices. A transparent four-signal screen repeatedly surfaces the same offices (Batangas 4th, Bulacan 1st, Sorsogon, Abra, Metro Manila 3rd). Are project-level records for these offices being pulled, and what explains realignments running to two-thirds of an office’s enacted budget, every year?

Underlying data: DBM-published NEP, GAA, veto messages, and allotment/obligation records, FY 1988–2026, compiled by AJ Montesa / People’s Budget Coalition. DPWH execution (SAAODB) is not available at the program/activity/project level, so this review covers appropriations — the long-run series, program composition, the 2019 line-item veto, the Unprogrammed Appropriations, and the office-by-office NEP-to-GAA record. “Realignment” denotes the arithmetic GAA − NEP difference and is not, on its own, evidence of irregularity. Prepared as a long-form companion to the DPWH budget briefing.
